prompt: A major key signature of four sharps belongs to which key?
explanation: Four sharps are F‚ôØ C‚ôØ G‚ôØ D‚ôØ. The last sharp (D‚ôØ) is the leading note, so the key is a semitone above it: E major.

prompt: What is the relative minor of A major?
explanation: The relative minor starts on the 6th degree of the major scale ‚Äî a minor 3rd below the tonic. A minor 3rd below A is F‚ôØ, so F‚ôØ minor.

prompt: In a harmonic minor scale, which degree is raised compared with the key signature?
explanation: The harmonic minor raises only the 7th degree (both ascending and descending).

prompt: How many flats are in the key signature of A‚ô≠ major?
explanation: A‚ô≠ major has B‚ô≠ E‚ô≠ A‚ô≠ D‚ô≠ ‚Äî four flats. (The second-to-last flat, A‚ô≠, names the key.)

prompt: A major key‚Äôs relative minor begins on which note, relative to the tonic?
explanation: The relative minor is a minor 3rd below the major tonic (the 6th degree of the major scale).

prompt: The note a chromatic semitone above C is:
explanation: A chromatic semitone keeps the same letter name, so C ‚Üí C‚ôØ (C ‚Üí D‚ô≠ would be a diatonic semitone).

prompt: C up to D‚ô≠ is an example of a:
explanation: Two different letter names a semitone apart (C and D‚ô≠) form a diatonic semitone.

prompt: The 6th degree of a major or minor scale is called the:
explanation: Degrees: 1 tonic, 2 supertonic, 3 mediant, 4 subdominant, 5 dominant, 6 submediant, 7 leading note.

prompt: Which major key has six sharps?
explanation: F‚ôØ major has F‚ôØ C‚ôØ G‚ôØ D‚ôØ A‚ôØ E‚ôØ ‚Äî six sharps. (C‚ôØ major has all seven.)

prompt: Ascending, the melodic minor scale raises which degrees?
explanation: Melodic minor raises the 6th and 7th going up, then cancels both (back to the key signature) coming down.

prompt: Which major key has just one flat?
explanation: F major has one flat (B‚ô≠). B‚ô≠ major has two flats; G major has one sharp.

prompt: The 5th degree of the scale is called the:
explanation: The 5th degree is the dominant ‚Äî the second most important note after the tonic.

prompt: The enharmonic equivalent of G‚ô≠ major is:
explanation: G‚ô≠ and F‚ôØ are the same pitch, so G‚ô≠ major (6 flats) sounds identical to F‚ôØ major (6 sharps).

prompt: The technical name for the 7th degree of a major scale is the:
explanation: The 7th degree is the leading note ‚Äî it "leads" up to the tonic.

prompt: How many sharps does B major have?
explanation: B major: F‚ôØ C‚ôØ G‚ôØ D‚ôØ A‚ôØ ‚Äî five sharps.

prompt: The relative minor of E‚ô≠ major is:
explanation: A minor 3rd below E‚ô≠ is C, so the relative minor is C minor (same three flats).

prompt: Which major key has three flats?
explanation: E‚ô≠ major has B‚ô≠ E‚ô≠ A‚ô≠ ‚Äî three flats. (C minor shares these but is a minor key.)

prompt: C‚ôØ major is enharmonically equivalent to:
explanation: C‚ôØ (7 sharps) and D‚ô≠ (5 flats) are the same pitch, so the keys sound identical.

prompt: In G‚ôØ harmonic minor, the raised 7th degree is written as:
explanation: The 7th degree of G‚ôØ minor is F‚ôØ; raising it a semitone for the harmonic minor gives F double sharp (FùÑ™).

prompt: The chromatic scale is made up entirely of:
explanation: A chromatic scale moves by semitone throughout, using every note.

prompt: The submediant of A major is the note:
explanation: The submediant is the 6th degree; the 6th note of A major is F‚ôØ.

prompt: Which minor key has just one sharp in its key signature?
explanation: E minor is the relative minor of G major, which has one sharp (F‚ôØ).

prompt: Which major key has six flats?
explanation: G‚ô≠ major has B‚ô≠ E‚ô≠ A‚ô≠ D‚ô≠ G‚ô≠ C‚ô≠ ‚Äî six flats. (It is the enharmonic of F‚ôØ major.)
